question_text: A solar eclipse occurs when the Moon blocks sunlight and casts a shadow on Earth. During a solar eclipse, the Sun appears to disappear from the daytime sky. Explain why solar eclipses are rarer than lunar eclipses, even though the Moon orbits Earth every 27.3 days.
correct_answer: Solar eclipses are rarer because the Moon's shadow is small and only falls on a narrow path on Earth's surface. Also, solar eclipses can only happen during a new Moon, but the Moon's orbit is tilted about 5° to Earth's orbit, so alignment is rare. Lunar eclipses are visible from the entire night side of Earth.
explanation: Although new Moons happen roughly every 29.5 days, the Moon's orbital plane is tilted 5° relative to Earth's orbital plane. This means most new Moons miss Earth's shadow entirely. When a solar eclipse does occur, the Moon's shadow is small (about 100-200 km wide), so it only touches a thin strip of Earth. By contrast, during a lunar eclipse, Earth's much larger shadow can cover the entire Moon, visible from half the planet.
